name: OWASP Top 10 (2021)
description: The OWASP Top 10 is a standard awareness document for developers and web application security. It represents a broad consensus about the most critical security risks to web applications. The 2021 edition includes three new categories, four categories with naming and scoping changes, and some consolidation from the 2017 edition. Organizations should adopt this document and start the process of ensuring that their web applications minimize these risks.

controls.title: Broken Access Control
controls.description: Access control enforces policy such that users cannot act outside of their intended permissions. Failures typically lead to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction of data, or performing a business function outside the user's limits. This category moved up from the fifth position in the 2017 edition, reflecting a significant increase in incidence across tested applications.
controls.discussion: Implement proper access control mechanisms using a deny-by-default approach, except for public resources. Enforce record ownership rather than accepting that the user can create, read, update, or delete any record. Minimize CORS usage, disable web server directory listing, ensure file metadata and backup files are not present within web roots, and log access control failures with alerting to administrators when appropriate.

controls.title: Cryptographic Failures
controls.description: This category, previously known as Sensitive Data Exposure, focuses on failures related to cryptography which often lead to exposure of sensitive data. This includes use of weak or outdated cryptographic algorithms, improper key management, lack of encryption for data in transit or at rest, and insufficient randomness. Cryptographic failures can result in the compromise of personal data, credentials, health records, financial information, and other protected data.
controls.discussion: Classify data processed, stored, or transmitted by an application and identify which data is sensitive according to privacy laws, regulatory requirements, or business needs. Apply controls as per the classification, and do not store sensitive data unnecessarily. Ensure up-to-date and strong standard algorithms, protocols, and keys are in place, and encrypt all data in transit with secure protocols such as TLS with forward secrecy.

controls.title: Injection
controls.description: Injection flaws occur when an application sends untrusted data to an interpreter as part of a command or query, including SQL, NoSQL, OS command, ORM, LDAP, and Expression Language injection. Cross-site scripting (XSS) has been merged into this category in the 2021 edition. An attacker can exploit injection vulnerabilities to execute unintended commands, access data without proper authorization, or modify or delete data.
controls.discussion: Use a safe API that avoids using the interpreter entirely, provides a parameterized interface, or migrates to Object Relational Mapping tools (ORMs). Use positive server-side input validation, and for any residual dynamic queries, escape special characters using the specific escape syntax for that interpreter. Apply LIMIT and other SQL controls within queries to prevent mass disclosure of records in case of injection.

controls.title: Insecure Design
controls.description: Insecure design is a broad category representing different weaknesses expressed as missing or ineffective control design. It is distinguished from insecure implementation — a secure design can still have implementation defects, but an insecure design cannot be fixed by a perfect implementation alone. This is a new category for 2021 that focuses on risks related to design and architectural flaws, calling for greater use of threat modeling, secure design patterns, and reference architectures.
controls.discussion: Establish and use a secure development lifecycle with application security professionals to help evaluate and design security and privacy-related controls. Use threat modeling for critical authentication, access control, business logic, and key flows. Integrate security language and controls into user stories, and integrate plausibility checks at each tier of the application from the frontend to the backend.

controls.title: Security Misconfiguration
controls.description: Security misconfiguration is the most commonly seen issue, often a result of insecure default configurations, incomplete or ad-hoc configurations, open cloud storage, misconfigured HTTP headers, or verbose error messages containing sensitive information. This category now includes the former XML External Entities (XXE) category. Without proper security hardening across the entire application stack, attackers can gain unauthorized access or knowledge of the system.
controls.discussion: Implement a repeatable hardening process that makes it fast and easy to deploy another environment that is properly locked down. Development, QA, and production environments should all be configured identically with different credentials used in each. Remove or do not install unused features and frameworks, and review and update the configurations appropriate to all security notes, updates, and patches as part of the patch management process.

controls.title: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
controls.description: This category covers the use of components such as libraries, frameworks, and other software modules that have known vulnerabilities or are no longer maintained. Applications and APIs using components with known vulnerabilities may undermine application defenses and enable various attacks. The risk is amplified if the organization does not know the versions of all components it uses, if software is unsupported or out of date, or if developers do not test the compatibility of updated libraries.
controls.discussion: Remove unused dependencies, unnecessary features, components, files, and documentation. Continuously inventory the versions of both client-side and server-side components and their dependencies using tools like OWASP Dependency-Check or retire.js. Monitor sources like CVE and NVD for vulnerabilities in the components, and use software composition analysis tools to automate the process. Only obtain components from official sources over secure links, and prefer signed packages.

controls.title: Identification and Authentication Failures
controls.description: Previously known as Broken Authentication, this category covers weaknesses in the confirmation of the user's identity, authentication, and session management. These failures can allow attackers to compromise passwords, keys, or session tokens, or to exploit other implementation flaws to assume other users' identities temporarily or permanently. Automated attacks such as credential stuffing and brute force are common attack vectors in this category.
controls.discussion: Implement multi-factor authentication where possible to prevent automated credential stuffing, brute force, and stolen credential reuse attacks. Do not ship or deploy with any default credentials, particularly for admin users. Implement weak password checks against a list of the top 10,000 worst passwords, and ensure registration, credential recovery, and API pathways are hardened against account enumeration attacks. Limit or increasingly delay failed login attempts and log all failures.

controls.title: Software and Data Integrity Failures
controls.description: This category focuses on making assumptions related to software updates, critical data, and CI/CD pipelines without verifying integrity. It includes insecure deserialization from the 2017 edition. This covers scenarios where an application relies upon plugins, libraries, or modules from untrusted sources, repositories, or content delivery networks, and where an insecure CI/CD pipeline can introduce the potential for unauthorized access, malicious code, or system compromise.
controls.discussion: Use digital signatures or similar mechanisms to verify the software or data is from the expected source and has not been altered. Ensure libraries and dependencies, such as npm or Maven, are consuming trusted repositories, and consider hosting an internal known-good repository that has been vetted. Ensure that a code review process is in place for code and configuration changes to minimize the chance that malicious code or configuration could be introduced into the software pipeline.

controls.title: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
controls.description: This category helps detect, escalate, and respond to active breaches. Without sufficient logging and monitoring, breaches cannot be detected in a timely manner. Insufficient logging, detection, monitoring, and active response occurs any time auditable events such as logins, failed logins, and high-value transactions are not logged, warnings and errors generate no or inadequate log messages, or logs are only stored locally.
controls.discussion: Ensure all login, access control, and server-side input validation failures can be logged with sufficient user context to identify suspicious or malicious accounts, and held for sufficient time to allow delayed forensic analysis. Ensure that logs are generated in a format that log management solutions can easily consume, and that high-value transactions have an audit trail with integrity controls to prevent tampering or deletion. Establish or adopt an incident response and recovery plan.

controls.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
controls.description: SSRF flaws occur whenever a web application fetches a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL. It allows an attacker to coerce the application to send a crafted request to an unexpected destination, even when protected by a firewall, VPN, or another type of network access control list. This is a new addition to the Top 10 for 2021, added based on the security community survey as an area of growing concern with modern cloud architectures.
controls.discussion: Segment remote resource access functionality in separate networks to reduce the impact of SSRF, and enforce deny-by-default firewall policies or network access control rules to block all but essential intranet traffic. Sanitize and validate all client-supplied input data, and do not send raw responses to clients. Disable HTTP redirections, and be aware of URL consistency to avoid attacks such as DNS rebinding and time-of-check-time-of-use (TOCTOU) race conditions.
